data.title: A Look At Region Group’s Valuation As It Completes And Extends Its Share Buyback Program
data.description:
Region Group (ASX:RGN) has completed its previously announced on market share buyback, repurchasing 14,100,000 shares for A$33.1 million, and extended the program’s duration through April 2, 2027, underscoring ongoing capital management efforts.

See our latest analysis for Region Group.

At a latest share price of A$2.23, Region Group’s recent 1-day and 7-day share price returns in positive territory contrast with weaker 90 day and year to date share price returns. The 1 year total shareholder return remains positive, suggesting near term momentum has eased after a stronger period for patient holders.

If this kind of capital management story has you looking wider, it could be a good moment to scan other ideas through the 4 top founder-led companies

With Region Group trading at A$2.23 alongside an estimated intrinsic value gap and a discount to analyst price targets, the question is whether this REIT is quietly undervalued or if the market is already pricing in future growth.

Advertisement

Price to Earnings of 8.2x: Is it justified?

Region Group is trading on a P/E of 8.2x, which screens as good value against both its estimated fair P/E of 13.5x and the global retail REITs average of 13x, even though it sits above the closer peer group average of 7.1x.

The P/E ratio compares the share price to earnings per share, so it reflects what investors are willing to pay today for each dollar of current earnings. For a REIT like Region Group, where earnings are tied to rental income from long leases with grocery anchored centres, this metric is often a quick way to gauge how the market is pricing its cash generating power.

In this context, the current 8.2x multiple is lower than the SWS fair P/E estimate of 13.5x. This is a level the market could move towards if sentiment or cash flow expectations were to converge with that fair ratio. Against the wider global retail REITs group at 13x, Region Group screens as cheaper. However, compared with its more direct peer average of 7.1x it looks a little richer. This suggests investors may already be paying a premium versus local peers while still sitting at a discount to broader REIT valuations and the modelled fair P/E.

While the 8.2x P/E suggests Region Group looks inexpensive against its fair ratio of 13.5x, the SWS DCF model goes a step further and puts fair value at A$2.91 per share versus the current A$2.23. That 23.4% discount raises a simple question: is this a value gap or a value trap for you.

data.title: Does Permian Basin Royalty Trust’s (PBT) Lower Payout Signal a Shift in Its Income Narrative?
data.description:

  • The trust also announced a reduced cash distribution of US$0.010662 per unit, highlighting how weaker operating results are flowing directly through to unitholder payouts.
  • We will now examine how the weaker annual earnings and reduced cash distribution shape Permian Basin Royalty Trust’s investment narrative for investors.

What Is Permian Basin Royalty Trust's Investment Narrative?

For someone holding Permian Basin Royalty Trust, the big-picture belief has always been straightforward: you are essentially betting on the cash that can be pulled out of a fixed pool of oil and gas interests, rather than on a traditional growth story. The sharp drop in 2025 revenue and earnings, together with March’s reduced US$0.010662 distribution, brings that into clearer focus by reminding investors how closely payouts track underlying production and pricing. In the short term, the main catalysts now look less about headline performance and more about whether monthly distributions stabilize after this weaker year, and how the SoftVest-driven governance push might eventually influence decisions around the trust’s terms. At the same time, the recent unit price strength suggests the market was not blindsided, so the earnings miss may not fully reset the story, but it does elevate the risk that today’s high multiple is baking in a lot of optimism about future checks in the mail.

However, one particular risk around the sustainability and volatility of those distributions is worth unpacking further for investors. Permian Basin Royalty Trust's shares are on the way up, but could they be overextended? Uncover how much higher they are than fair value.

data.title: Morningstar, Inc. (NASDAQ:MORN) Passed Our Checks, And It's About To Pay A US$0.50 Dividend
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see Morningstar, Inc. (NASDAQ:MORN) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next two days. Typically, the ex-dividend date is one business day before the record date, which is the date on which a company determines the shareholders eligible to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is an important date to be aware of as any purchase of the stock made on or after this date might mean a late settlement that doesn't show on the record date. Therefore, if you purchase Morningstar's shares on or after the 2nd of April, you won't be eligible to receive the dividend, when it is paid on the 30th of April.

The company's next dividend payment will be US$0.50 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of US$2.00 to shareholders. Based on the last year's worth of payments, Morningstar stock has a trailing yield of around 1.3% on the current share price of US$156.81. Dividends are an important source of income to many shareholders, but the health of the business is crucial to maintaining those dividends. That's why we should always check whether the dividend payments appear sustainable, and if the company is growing.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Dividends are typically paid from company earnings. If a company pays more in dividends than it earned in profit, then the dividend could be unsustainable. Morningstar is paying out just 21% of its profit after tax, which is comfortably low and leaves plenty of breathing room in the case of adverse events.

Companies that pay out less in dividends than they earn in profits generally have more sustainable dividends. The lower the payout ratio, the more wiggle room the business has before it could be forced to cut the dividend.

View our latest analysis for Morningstar

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. If earnings fall far enough, the company could be forced to cut its dividend. For this reason, we're glad to see Morningstar's earnings per share have risen 13% per annum over the last five years.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. In the last 10 years, Morningstar has lifted its dividend by approximately 10% a year on average. It's great to see earnings per share growing rapidly over several years, and dividends per share growing right along with it.

To Sum It Up

From a dividend perspective, should investors buy or avoid Morningstar? When companies are growing rapidly and retaining a majority of the profits within the business, it's usually a sign that reinvesting earnings creates more value than paying dividends to shareholders. This strategy can add significant value to shareholders over the long term - as long as it's done without issuing too many new shares. Morningstar ticks a lot of boxes for us from a dividend perspective, and we think these characteristics should mark the company as deserving of further attention.

While it's tempting to invest in Morningstar for the dividends alone, you should always be mindful of the risks involved. Case in point: We've spotted 1 warning sign for Morningstar you should be aware of.

data.title: We Think That There Are Issues Underlying Alvopetro Energy's (CVE:ALV) Earnings
data.description:
Alvopetro Energy Ltd.'s (CVE:ALV) stock was strong after they recently reported robust earnings. However, we think that shareholders may be missing some concerning details in the numbers.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Zooming In On Alvopetro Energy's Earnings

As finance nerds would already know, the accrual ratio from cashflow is a key measure for assessing how well a company's free cash flow (FCF) matches its profit. The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

That means a negative accrual ratio is a good thing, because it shows that the company is bringing in more free cash flow than its profit would suggest. While having an accrual ratio above zero is of little concern, we do think it's worth noting when a company has a relatively high accrual ratio. To quote a 2014 paper by Lewellen and Resutek, "firms with higher accruals tend to be less profitable in the future".

Over the twelve months to December 2025, Alvopetro Energy recorded an accrual ratio of 0.22. Unfortunately, that means its free cash flow fell significantly short of its reported profits. To wit, it produced free cash flow of US$7.3m during the period, falling well short of its reported profit of US$23.1m. Alvopetro Energy shareholders will no doubt be hoping that its free cash flow bounces back next year, since it was down over the last twelve months. One positive for Alvopetro Energy shareholders is that it's accrual ratio was significantly better last year, providing reason to believe that it may return to stronger cash conversion in the future. Shareholders should look for improved cashflow relative to profit in the current year, if that is indeed the case.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

Our Take On Alvopetro Energy's Profit Performance

Alvopetro Energy didn't convert much of its profit to free cash flow in the last year, which some investors may consider rather suboptimal. Therefore, it seems possible to us that Alvopetro Energy's true underlying earnings power is actually less than its statutory profit. The good news is that, its earnings per share increased by 42% in the last year. The goal of this article has been to assess how well we can rely on the statutory earnings to reflect the company's potential, but there is plenty more to consider. If you want to do dive deeper into Alvopetro Energy, you'd also look into what risks it is currently facing. For example, Alvopetro Energy has 2 warning signs (and 1 which is significant) we think you should know about.

Today we've zoomed in on a single data point to better understand the nature of Alvopetro Energy's profit. But there is always more to discover if you are capable of focussing your mind on minutiae. data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Indivior Pharmaceuticals (INDV) After Its 200% One Year Surge?
data.description:
• For readers wondering whether Indivior Pharmaceuticals at around US$28.97 is still a good entry point or more of a hold after such a big run, this article focuses squarely on what the current valuation is indicating.
  • The stock has pulled back, with a 1.4% decline over the last 7 days, an 11.5% decline over 30 days, and an 18.9% decline year to date. This follows a very strong 204.0% return over 1 year and 216.6% over 5 years.
  • Recent attention around Indivior Pharmaceuticals has centered on how the market is reassessing companies in the pharmaceuticals and biotech space, and how that reassessment shows up in prices after large multi year moves. This context helps explain why a stock with a 72.9% 3 year return and a 204.0% 1 year return can still experience shorter term pullbacks.
  • Indivior Pharmaceuticals currently scores 5 out of 6 on Simply Wall St's valuation checks. The next sections will walk through the different valuation methods behind that score and will also outline an even more detailed way to think about value at the end of the article.

Indivior Pharmaceuticals delivered 204.0% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Pharmaceuticals industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Indivior Pharmaceuticals Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash a business may generate in the future and discounts those back into today’s dollars to arrive at an estimate of intrinsic value per share.

For Indivior Pharmaceuticals, the DCF model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $80.2 million, so the model relies heavily on projections. Analyst estimates and Simply Wall St extrapolations point to free cash flow reaching $536.5 million in 2030, with annual figures between 2026 and 2035 ranging from $379.1 million to $656.3 million in projected free cash flow.

Discounting these projected cash flows back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $109.43 per share. Compared with a current share price around $28.97, the model suggests Indivior Pharmaceuticals trades at a 73.5% discount to this DCF estimate. This represents a wide gap for readers who rely on cash flow based valuation.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Indivior Pharmaceuticals is undervalued by 73.5%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 62 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Indivior Pharmaceuticals.

Approach 2: Indivior Pharmaceuticals Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for a share to the earnings that each share generates. It helps you see how many years of current earnings the market is pricing in, which is often how investors quickly compare opportunities.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on what the market expects for growth and how risky those earnings appear. Higher expected growth or perceived resilience can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually lines up with a lower P/E.

Indivior Pharmaceuticals currently trades on a P/E of 16.8x. That sits close to the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 16.7x and below the peer group average of 34.9x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for the stock is 21.5x, which is its proprietary view of what the P/E could be given factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risks. This Fair Ratio can be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it adjusts for those company level characteristics. Comparing the current P/E of 16.8x with the Fair Ratio of 21.5x points to the shares trading below that Fair Ratio input.

Result: UNDERVALUED
